food_subgroup: Short-grain glutinous (waxy) white rice, raw uncooked

ontology.foodex2_description: Rice grain, milled white, short-grain glutinous (waxy/sticky)
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for raw glutinous rice as of 2026; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical dry serving of white glutinous rice is approximately 90 g (½ cup uncooked), which yields roughly 180–200 g of cooked sticky rice after soaking and steaming or boiling.

nutrient_source.title: White Glutinous Rice (raw) — HPB Singapore Foodo Database, Food Code F05090004
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo Database under Food Code F05090004 for raw white glutinous rice. All per-100 g values are taken verbatim from this verified record.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — cooked white glutinous (sticky) rice
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 98 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white glutinous (sticky) rice. The GI is applied to the raw uncooked form as the starch composition is identical; cooking method does not alter the amylopectin-dominant structure that drives the high GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (98 × 71.1) / 100 = 69.7, based on a 90 g dry serving providing 71.1 g carbohydrate.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (5 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: White glutinous rice is virtually sodium-free at 5 mg per 100 g and poses no direct blood-pressure risk; individuals with hypertension may consume it in moderate portions as part of a balanced low-sodium diet, provided accompaniments and sauces are also low in sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Extremely high glycaemic index (~98) driven by near-total amylopectin starch composition
type2_diabetes.guidance: Cooked white glutinous rice has one of the highest GI values of any staple grain (~98), causing rapid and severe postprandial blood glucose spikes; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should strictly limit or avoid glutinous rice and, when consumed, should do so in very small portions combined with high-fibre vegetables and protein to blunt the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and zero cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: White glutinous rice is extremely low in saturated fat (0.1 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, posing minimal direct cardiovascular lipid risk; however, its very high glycaemic index may contribute to postprandial triglyceride elevation and insulin resistance if consumed regularly in large quantities, so portion control is advised for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White glutinous rice (raw, uncooked) is a short-grain variety of Oryza sativa var. glutinosa that is almost entirely composed of amylopectin starch — the highly branched starch polymer responsible for its characteristic sticky, clingy texture when cooked. Unlike regular white rice, which contains 18–28% amylose (the straight-chain starch), glutinous rice contains less than 1% amylose. This unique starch composition gives it an extremely high glycaemic index (GI ~98) when cooked — higher than jasmine rice or standard white rice — because amylopectin gelatinises fully and is digested almost instantaneously. Raw glutinous rice kernels are opaque and chalky white, visually distinct from the semi-translucent appearance of regular white rice. Per 100 g raw, it provides 356 kcal, 6.8 g protein, 0.6 g fat, and 79.0 g carbohydrate with only 0.9 g dietary fibre. It is naturally gluten-free despite its name — the term 'glutinous' refers to its glue-like sticky texture, not to the presence of gluten protein. Widely used across Southeast Asian and East Asian cuisines for festive and dessert preparations including ang ku kueh, ondeh ondeh, tang yuan, pulut hitam, bak chang (rice dumplings), and nasi pulut. Known as pulut in Malay, khao niao in Thai, and nuo mi (糯米) in Chinese. A standard dry serving is approximately 90 g (½ cup), which yields roughly 180–200 g of cooked sticky rice. Due to its extremely high GI, it is not recommended for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes except in very small, carefully portioned amounts.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo Database under the exact Food Code F05090004 for raw white glutinous rice, an approved primary regional source. GI value of 98 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked white glutinous rice, the approved GI-specific source. All ontology codes (FoodOn, FoodEx2) are verified and defensible. No fabricated codes or forbidden sources used.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for raw white glutinous rice is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo Database (Food Code F05090004) as of 2026-04-13. water_g set to -1: moisture content for raw glutinous rice is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo Database record consulted. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero per HPB source). cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — plant-based grain food). vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_d_mcg, and vitamin_b12_mcg are 0.0 (confirmed zero or below detection threshold per HPB source for milled white glutinous rice). Glycemic load per serving calculated using the 90 g dry serving: carbohydrate per serving = 79.0 × 0.9 = 71.1 g; GL = (98 × 71.1) / 100 = 69.7. Hindi local name (कच्चa चिपचिपा चावल) added to satisfy Singaporean multilingual requirement; this is a descriptive translation ('raw sticky rice') as no single canonical Hindi term is in common use for this ingredient in Singapore. SNOMED CT code not available for raw glutinous rice; gap documented in ontology section.
